coredata.dc:title: Effectively enhancing red fluorescence strategy and bioimaging applications of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fanghao
Dong, Xuezhe
Zuo, Yujing
Xie, Zheng
Guan, Ruifang
coredata.dc:description: Red-emitting carbon dots have attracted much attention because of their excellent fluorescence properties. It is of great significance to synthesize red-emitting carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield. Herein, by using citric acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors in N, N-dimethylformamide, novel carbon dots (NC-CDs) exhibiting red emission at 607 nm upon excitation at 514 nm were synthesized via a solvent-thermal approach. NC-CDs aggregated in lipid droplets of cells, and hemin quenched their fluorescence. Subsequently, cationic surfactants cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, anionic surfactants sodium dodecyl sulfate and nonionic surfactants were used to functionalize the NC-CDs. The results indicated that both ionic surfactants increased the fluorescence quantum yield and solubility of NC-CDs with extended fluorescence lifetime. This approach is applicable to a class of carbon dots that possess both carboxyl and amino groups. Additionally, these functionalized NC-CDs exhibited high recognition sensitivity towards ClO− and Cu2+ respectively except for hemin. This study provides a novel approach for the synthesis of highly red-emitting carbon dots, expanding their applications in the field of biological imaging and sensing. The findings hold significant implications for the real-time monitoring of hemin in lipid droplets. Additionally, this work offers a simple method to dramatic enhance their fluorescence quantum yield.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red-emitting carbon dots
Enhancing fluorescence
Surfactant functionalization
Bioimaging
Ions recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence imaging and hemin detection of NC-CDs
Ions detection of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21875267 , 22274064 , 22275202 , 22007037 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of NC-CDs.

(a) TEM image of NC-CDs (insets showing the particle size distribution). (b) HR-TEM image of NC-CDs, inset: lattice spacing (marked with red circles). (c) XRD image of NC-CDs and (d) Raman spectrum. (e) XPS spectrum of. NC-CDs. (Illustration: percentage of elements in NC-CDs) and high-resolution XPS C1s spectrum (f), N1s spectrum (g) and O1s spectrum (h) of NC-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of NC-CDs, the red line represents the absorption spectrum, the blue line represents the excitation spectrum, and the black line represents the emission spectrum, inset: photographs of NC-CDs under ambient light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (b) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (c) Fluorescence lifetime spectrum. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

TEM images and size distribution of (a) (b) CDs/CTAB and (d) (e) CDs/SDS, HR-TEM images of (c) CDs/CTAB and (f) CDs/SDS, inset: lattice spacing (marked with red circles). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The mechanism diagram enhanced fluorescence performance of NC-CDs with cationic/ionic surfactant.

Quenching effect of NC-CDs by (a) amino acids and (b) ions, (c) linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and hemin concentration, (d) confocal fluorescence imaging of Hela cells treated with NC-CDs (5 μg/mL) for 2 h, λex = 488 nm, and (e) images of Hela cells stimulated with hemin with different concentration.

Quenching effect of CDs/CTAB by (a) amino acid and (c) ion, (b) linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and hemin concentration, (d) linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and ClO− concentration; Quenching effect of CDs/SDS by (e) amino acid and (g) ion, (f) linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and hemin concentration, (h) linear relationship between (F0 − F)/F0 and Cu2+ concentration.
Different methods to enhance the QY of CDs.

head.ce:title: Effectively enhancing red fluorescence strategy and bioimaging applications of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong, 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emitting carbon dots have attracted much attention because of their excellent fluorescence properties. It is of great significance to synthesize red-emitting carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield. Herein, by using citric acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors in N, N-dimethylformamide, novel carbon dots (NC-CDs) exhibiting red emission at 607 nm upon excitation at 514 nm were synthesized via a solvent-thermal approach. NC-CDs aggregated in lipid droplets of cells, and hemin quenched their fluorescence. Subsequently, cationic surfactants cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, anionic surfactants sodium dodecyl sulfate and nonionic surfactants were used to functionalize the NC-CDs. The results indicated that both ionic surfactants increased the fluorescence quantum yield and solubility of NC-CDs with extended fluorescence lifetime. This approach is applicable to a class of carbon dots that possess both carboxyl and amino groups. Additionally, these functionalized NC-CDs exhibited high recognition sensitivity towards ClO− and Cu2+ respectively except for hemin. This study provides a novel approach for the synthesis of highly red-emitting carbon dots, expanding their applications in the field of biological imaging and sensing. The findings hold significant implications for the real-time monitoring of hemin in lipid droplets. Additionally, this work offers a simple method to dramatic enhance their fluorescence quantum yield.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have received widespread attention in recent years due to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high quantum yield (QY). They have shown tremendous potential in various applications such as biological imaging, optoelectronic devices, energy, sensing, and drug delivery [1]. Fluorescent imaging technique possesses unique advantages in monitoring intracellular species due to its high sensitivity, real-time imaging capabilities, and minimal detrimental effects [2,3]. However, most of the reported CDs exhibit intense blue-green luminescence under excitation by UV light, and have relatively low QY in the long wavelength range [4,5]. These drawbacks severely restrict their potential applications, particularly in the field of biology applications, due to the strong autofluorescence of tissues at low wavelength emissions and the potential tissue damage due to ultraviolet light exposure [6,7]. Red-emitting CDs are suitable for cell and tissue imaging due to their deep tissue penetration capability, minimal sample damage, and minimal background autofluorescence from biological samples [8–10]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop simple and efficient methods for synthesizing red-emitting CDs. Aniline [11–14] and phenol [15,16] were frequently employed in the synthesis. Furthermore, in our previous research, many long-wavelength emission CDs were successfully synthesized using 1,4-diaminonaphthalene, fused rings and phthalocyanines, etc [17,18]. Nevertheless, a significant challenge arising from these achievements is the poor water solubility exhibited by red-light emitting CDs, which holds particular importance, especially for long-wavelength CDs. However, it is uncertain whether larger conjugated structures might be more effective to produce long-wavelength emissions [19].

In addition, the synthesis of red-emitting CDs with high fluorescence QY holds immense significance. Common methods to improve the QY are categorized as follows: optimizing reaction conditions, introducing catalysts, surface modification to regulate the morphology and properties of CDs, and improving purification and separation techniques. For instance, Zheng et al. employed NaBH4 reduction to prepare reduced CDs, resulting in an increase in the QY of CDs from 2 % to 24 % [20]; Sato et al. performed surface modification of CDs using 4-morpholinoaniline with electron-donating groups and rigid structures, followed by purification through silica gel column chromatography, achieving an improvement in QY from 28.3 % to 41.8 % [21]; Liang et al. enhanced the QY of CDs from 36 % to 42 %, 61 %, and 68 % through acid treatment, element doping, and passivation treatments, respectively [22]. In our previous work, we successfully increased the QY from 47 % in silane-functionalized CDs’ solution to 88 % in their solid states by copolymerizing and curing with organsilanes to form gel glasses [23]. However, the most of these methods are all chemical methods, which involve complex operating procedures and are prone to impurity introduction, requiring further purification in subsequent steps.

Herein, 1,8-diaminonaphthalene with a larger conjugated structure was used to synthesize novel CDs (NC-CDs) via a solvothermal method. The obtained NC-CDs exhibited an emission peak at 607 nm under 514 nm excitation. The red fluorescence of NC-CDs can be observed in lipid droplets, and gradually disappearing with addition of hemin. To enhance CDs’ fluorescence intensity, three kinds of surfactants were applied: cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) and nonionic surfactants. The QYs of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS all increase with an extended fluorescence lifetime. Moreover, CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS exhibited new high selectivity and sensitivity towards ClO− and Cu2+ respectively. Moreover, by investigating various types of CDs, it was ultimately discovered that a specific class of CDs possessing both amino and carboxyl groups could be successfully applied with this method, thereby enhancing their fluorescence performance. So, these NC-CDs can be used for cell imaging and detection of hemin in cells, and this study provides a simple and effective approach for improving QY, lifetime and solubility of Red and other longer wavelength emission CDs.

1,8-diaminonaphthalene, citric acid, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), SDS, CTAB, and inorganic salts were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used in all experiments. All chemicals used in this work are of analytical grade and need no further purification.

UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra, QY and lifetime were recorded using a Horiba FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained with a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out by X-ray diffractometer (SmartLab 9 KW). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired by the X-ray spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were taken by a Nicolet380 FT-IR spectrometer. The height of CDs was measured by atomic force microscope (AFM, Asylum Research, ARC2). Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells was performed with a Nikon A1MP confocal microscope. Zeta potential were measured using a nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS9, UK).

NC-CDs were synthesized by a solvothermal method as follows. 30 mg (0.16 mmol) critic acid and 60 mg (0.38 mmol) 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were dissolved in 30 mL DMF and ultrasonically dispersed evenly. The solution was transferred to 50 mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. Being cooled to room temperature, the product was separated by silica gel column to obtain red solution, which was dried to obtain the NC-CDs. The synthetic route of NC-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 .

The NC-CDs powder (4 mg) was added into a beaker with 100 mL of deionized water, ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min, 0.364 g (1 mmol) CTAB was added and stirred for 6 h at room temperature, then dialyzed to remove excess surfactant to obtain the aqueous solution of CDs/CTAB.
Repeat the above steps, the surfactant SDS rather than CTAB was used, then CDs/SDS aqueous solution was obtained.

First, the impact of various reaction solvents on the synthesis of NC-CDs was investigated. Table S1 listed the results of using various organic solvents separately, including formamide, DMSO, acetonitrile, DMF, acetone, and toluene. Fluorescence spectra analysis revealed that the product obtained in DMF solution had emission at 607 nm under excitation at 514 nm. Although long-wavelength emission was also observed in the reaction using toluene solvent, its low intensity and toxicity made it unsuitable for further use. Therefore, DMF was chosen as the reaction solvent to prepare NC-CDs. In addition, systematic experiments were conducted to explore the optimal reaction temperature. Three temperatures (180 °C, 200 °C, and 220 °C) were selected for synthesis. The average sizes of the synthesized samples are 2.13 nm, 2.87 nm, and 2.53 nm, respectively. It was found that the sample synthesized at 200 °C exhibited the longest emission wavelength and the largest size (Figs. S1 and S2). The increase in the size of CDs may lead to a reduction in the bandgap, resulting in a gradual redshift of the emission wavelength [12]. Therefore, 200 °C was chosen as the final synthesis temperature for further investigation of NC-CDs.

Fig. 2 a shows the TEM image of monodisperse NC-CDs. The average diameter of spheroidal NC-CDs is 2.87 nm, which is consistent with AFM image shown in Fig. S3 with the particle size ranging from 2 to 4.5 nm. The HR-TEM image (inserted in Fig. 2b) shows lattice stripes with good resolution, and the crystal plane spacing is 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of graphite. The XRD image (Fig. 2c) shows a 2θ value of 24.1°, and the intensity ratio of D band to the G band in Raman spectrum is 0.59 (Fig. 2d), which shows that the prepared NC-CDs have a high degree of graphitization.

The XPS spectrum of NC-CDs (Fig. 2e) reveals that the content of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in NC-CDs is 80.9 %, 11.4 %, and 7.7 %, respectively. The C1s spectrum shows the presence of C=C (284.75 eV), C–N (285.65 eV), and C=O (287.91 eV), while the O1s spectrum confirms the existence of C=O (530.65 eV) and C–O (531.71 eV). The N1s spectrum displays three functional groups, namely pyridinic N (398.5 eV), pyrrolic N (399.7 eV), and graphitic N (400.4 eV) [24,25]. The appearance of pyridine N implies that the amino group of 1,8-diaminonaphthalene has reacted. Furthermore, the presence of graphite N indicates the involvement of N in the internal construction of the carbon core. FT-IR spectroscopy also reveals the presence of amide groups in the prepared NC-CDs, while retaining their conjugated structure, consistent with the XPS analysis results (Fig. S4a) [26].

UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra analysis revealed the presence of absorption peaks at 334 nm and 492 nm, with a maximum emission wavelength at 607 nm under excitation at 514 nm. The NC-CDs solution appeared as a red color under daylight, and emitted red fluorescence under 365 nm excitation (Fig. 3 a). As the excitation wavelength gradually increases from 440 nm to 560 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of NC-CDs initially increases and then decreases. However, the peak emission value remains constant (Fig. 3b). Therefore, the emission peak at 607 nm is attributed to the intrinsic structure of CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of NC-CDs is 0.46 ns (Fig. 3c). Experimental results demonstrate that red-emitting CDs are successfully synthesized using 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as the precursor. This is attributed to the greater conjugated structure of naphthalenediamine compared to benzenediamine, leading to a larger conjugated core of NC-CDs, and longer-wavelength fluorescence emission is observed [27]. These research findings provide new directions for precursor selection in the synthesis of red-emitting CDs.

Application of CDs is often limited by low QY and low water solubility [28,29]. Here, a method to improve the QY of CDs is presented in detail in section 2.3 . Cationic surfactant CTAB, anionic surfactant SDS, and nonionic surfactant Triton-100 were selected to functionalize NC-CDs. The NC-CDs were initially insoluble in water. The two solutions containing CTAB and SDS turned pink, and exhibited red fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm light (Fig. S5). Fig. S6 indicates that the fluorescence emission maximum occurs with a surfactant concentration of 10 mM. Meanwhile, the Triton-100 solution remained unchanged in color and showed hardly any fluorescence.

It was observed that the addition of two different surfactants improved the fluorescence lifetime and QY of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS. The lifetime was prolonged to 0.96 ns of CDs/CTAB, and to 0.84 ns for CDs/SDS as shown in Fig. S7. Notably, the QYs increase from 0.33 % (ethanol solution) to 13.45 % for CDs/CTAB, and to 30.68 % for CDs/SDS (nearly 100-fold). It is a simple and highly efficient strategy for improving the fluorescence lifetime and QY of CDs, as well as their water solubility (particularly crucial for long-wavelength CDs, as they often exhibit poor water solubility) through the addition of proper surfactants.

Fig. 4 a and d respectively demonstrate that CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS have good dispersibility in water, with average particle sizes of 2.82 nm and 2.90 nm, respectively. These sizes are almost the same as the size of the NC-CDs, and both CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS exhibit a lattice spacing 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (101) crystal plane of graphite. This is the same as the one of NC-CDs shown in Fig. 2. In addition, Fig. 3a and Fig. S8 illustrate that the fluorescence emission peak position of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS remain unaltered, indicating that the addition of surfactant modifing surface groups rather than the carbon core of NC-CDs.

Then, the mode of action and effects of cationic/ionic surfactant on NC-CDs was explored. Fig. 4 indicates that CDs/CTAB are uniformly dispersed with a relatively large distance between CDs, while CDs/SDS aggregate. Meanwhile, in Table S2, CDs/CTAB have a high zeta potential, indicating that more repulsion exists between functionalized particles. CTAB is a cationic surfactant that has polar hydrophilic groups and nonpolar hydrophobic groups and is adsorbed physically onto the surface of CDs [30]. The positive charge towards aqueous phase establishes a protective shield around CDs, which resists self-quenching or emission re-absorption [30], which prevents non-radiative energy losses around CDs. Then reducing the non-radiative dissipation of NC-CDs, further improving the fluorescence lifetime and QY [31]. For CDs/SDS, NC-CDs have a large amount of positive charge on their surface (Table S2), and SDS is an anionic surface-active agent. The electrostatic attraction between positively charged CDs and negatively charged SDS molecules occurs spontaneously. The charge neutralization of them causes agglomeration of CDs, which enhance the formation of charge transfer complexes between CDs, significantly reducing non-radiative energy dissipation, and thus the QY and lifetime were significantly improved (Fig. 5 ) [32].

To explore general applicability of the method, several other CDs samples were functionalized in the same way. CD-1 was synthesized through a solvothermal reaction of terephthalic acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene. CD-1 possesses a conjugated structure, surface functional groups (carboxyl and amino groups) similar to NC-CDs, as well as hydrophobic properties. Initially, CD-1 are insoluble in aqueous solutions, however more samples were successful dispersed into water with the emission of orange-red fluorescence (Fig. S5) after addition of surfactants. Furthermore, the fluorescence performance was enhanced (Table S3). CD-2∼CD-5 were synthesized from aniline, boronic acid and adipic acid, citric acid and naphthalenediamine, respectively. However, other CDs with surface functional groups consisting solely of amino or carboxyl groups do not exhibit the same effect after undergoing the same functionalization. Table 1 lists different methods to enhance the QY of CDs. The previously reported methods primarily involve complex chemical processes. This work presents a straightforward approach, stands out for its simplicity of operation, and has an obvious effect in increasing QY (nearly 100-fold increase).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging and hemin detection of NC-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We devised an assay for the assessment of over various common amino acids and ions by NC-CDs. Fig. 6 a shows that NC-CDs exhibit a specific response to hemin within 1 min (Fig. S9a). The fluorescence intensity of NC-CDs at 607 nm (excitation wavelength 514 nm) gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of hemin. The quenching efficiency ((F0 − F)/F0, y) exhibits a desired linear relationship with hemin concentration (x) (0.10–20.00 μM), given by the equation y = − 0.73*exp (−x/27.855) + 0.736, R2 = 0.997 (Fig. 6c). The limit of detection (LOD) of NC-CDs for hemin was calculated to be 0.183 μM. By comparing the absorption and lifetime spectra, it was observed that no new peaks in the absorption spectrum after the addition of hemin. However, the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 0.46 ns to 0.13 ns by adding hemin (Fig. S9). The results indicate that the addition of hemin caused the dynamic quenching of the NC-CDs fluorescence.

Furthermore, NC-CDs show great potential for bioimaging applications. As shown in Fig. 6d, when the cells were incubated with NC-CDs, a bright red fluorescence was observed in the lipid droplets of cells, owing to their excellent liposolubility. Fig. S10b displays the correlation profiles between the fluorescence intensity and the pixels of NC-CDs, BODIPY 493/503 along the yellow arrow marked in Fig. S10a, and their profiles are entirely consistent in locations. The Pearson correlation coefficient (Rr) is as high as 0.92, proving the excellent LD-targeting property of these NC-CDs.

Based on the specific recognition of hemin by NC-CDs, an experiment for cells responding to hemin was designed, as shown in Fig. 6e. Firstly, the blank group was incubated with NC-CDs for 1.5 h. As for the control group, 10, 20, and 200 μL of hemin were respectively added to stimulate the cells, and then incubated with NC-CDs for 1.5 h. The results showed that, compared to the blank group, the fluorescence intensity labeling in the control group weakened. Moreover, with the increase addition of hemin, the fluorescence intensity decreases significantly, and finally disappears (the fluorescence intensity values are shown as 153, 49, 34, 0.2, respectively). This method can provide a suitable approach to visualize the hemin content in lipid droplet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ions detection of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS
ce:section.ce:para: Surfactants can significantly change the microenvironment of the system and sensitize the fluorescence behavior of CDs [33,34]. After functionalization of NC-CDs, it was found that CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS retained their recognition of hemin (Fig. 7 a and b), with detection limits of 0.59 μM and 0.285 μM, respectively. The detection fitting curves are shown in Fig. 7b and f. In addition, we were happy to discover that CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS exhibited specific responses to ClO− and Cu2+ ions, respectively (Fig. 7c and g).

The fluorescence intensity of CDs/CTAB at 607 nm (excitation wavelength 514 nm) decreases gradually with increasing ClO− concentration. The quenching efficiency ((F0 − F)/F0, y) and ClO− concentration (x) (between 0.10–30.00 μM) has a good linear relationship (y = 0.0939 − 0.94*exp (−0.054*x), R2 = 0.99575), as illustrated in Fig. 7d. LOD of CDs/CTAB for ClO− is calculated as 0.079 μM. There are few reports on the detection of ClO− using red-emitting CDs. Compared to other ClO− detection methods using red-emitting CDs, the CDs/CTAB used in this experiment demonstrate higher QY and higher sensitivity (Table S4). The addition of CTAB not only increases the QY of CDs but also enhances their sensitivity for ion detection.

The absorption peak at 497 nm of CDs/CTAB was weakened with increment of ClO−, and the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 0.96 ns to 0.27 ns, as shown in Fig. S11. This indicates that a combination of dynamic and static quenching is the possible quenching mechanism. Since the peak position of CDs/CTAB remained unchanged, but the zeta potential showed a decrease after adding ClO− (Table S5), therefore, it is suspected that ClO− does not exert a direct impact on the carbon nuclei, but rather engages in charge-attraction interactions with the cationic moiety of CTAB, subsequently leading to its sequestration from the CDs/CTAB system.

CDs/SDS can also specifically recognize Cu2+ as shown in Fig. 7g. As the concentration of Cu2+ increases, the fluorescence intensity of CDs/SDS at 607 nm (excitation wavelength 514 nm) gradually decreases. The quenching efficiency ((F0 − F)/F0, y) shows a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration (x) in the range of 0.10–40.00 μM (y = 0.347*tanh (0.0119*x/Pm), R2 = 0.99274) (Fig. 7h). LOD of CDs/SDS for Cu2+ was calculated to be 1.29 μM. In comparison, CDs/SDS have shown great potential in the field of detection applications due to their higher QY and comparatively lower detection limit (Table S6).

Through analyzing the absorption spectra and lifetime spectra before and after the addition of Cu2+, we can see from Fig. S11 that the original absorption peak of CDs/SDS at 497 nm weakened after the addition of Cu2+, while the fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly. It can be inferred that the addition of ions caused static quenching, resulting in the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Moreover, combining the zeta potential before and after, there was no significant change in Table S5, so we can assume that Cu2+ did not react with the surface of CDs/SDS, but was captured within CDs.

The chemical stability, photo-stability, and response time of CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS are explored. Firstly, both CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS could achieve complete response within 1 min after adding the ions (Figs. S12a and e). Subsequently, as shown in Figs. S12b and f, the highest intensity was achieved at pH 7 which was chosen as the optimal testing condition for CDs/CTAB. When the pH is 6.8, the intensity of CDs/SDS reaches its maximum. Therefore, a pH value of 7 is considered appropriate for optimal testing conditions for CD/SDS. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of both samples remained almost constant after continuous exposure for 60 min (Figs. S12c and g). Finally, even with a NaCl concentration of 200.00 mM, the fluorescence intensity of both were relatively stable (Figs. S12d and h), demonstrating excellent salt tolerance. These results indicate that CDs/CTAB and CDs/SDS have excellent chemical and photo-stability, which are suitable for further applications.

In summary, NC-CDs were successfully prepared using 1,8-diaminonaphthalene and citric acid through a solvothermal method. The prepared NC-CDs can achieve the localization of lipid droplets in cell imaging, as well as detect hemin in cells. Surfactant functionalization of NC-CDs resulted in a nearly 100-fold increase in the QY, and this method is applicable to the same type of CDs. CTAB-functionalized CDs exhibited high sensitivity for ClO−, with a detection limit in the nanomolar range, while SDS-functionalized CDs showed high selectivity for Cu2+. Furthermore, the addition of surfactants leads to the dispersion or aggregation of CDs, which is the main reason for the enhancement of their photoluminescence performance. A simple and efficient route is proposed in this study for enhancing the performance and improving the solubility of red-emitting CDs. This method has significant potential to extend the application of CDs in biological and other fields.

Fanghao Wang: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization. Xuezhe Dong: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology. Yujing Zuo: Funding acquisition, Resources, Validation, Visualization. Zheng Xie: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Project administration. Ruifang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Supervision, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Zheng Xie reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Yujing Zuo reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 21875267, 22274064, 22275202, 22007037).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtphys.2024.101332.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, X.; Ray, R.; Gu, Y.; Ploehn, H. J.; Gearheart, L.; Raker, K.; Scrivens, W. A., Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments. Journal of the American Chemical Society 2004, 126 (40), 12736-12737.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A selective fluorescent sensor for imaging Cd2+ in living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Peng, X.; Du, J.; Fan, J.; Wang, J.; Wu, Y.; Zhao, J.; Sun, S.; Xu, T., A Selective Fluorescent Sensor for Imaging Cd2+ in Living Cells. Journal of the American Chemical Society 2007, 129 (6), 1500-1501.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer-integrated DNA nanostructures for biosensing, bioimaging and cancer therapy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Meng, H.-M.; Liu, H.; Kuai, H.; Peng, R.; Mo, L.; Zhang, X.-B., Aptamer-integrated DNA nanostructures for biosensing, bioimaging and cancer therapy. Chemical Society Reviews 2016, 45 (9), 2583-2602.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang, K.; Sun, S.; Zhang, L.; Lu, Y.; Wu, A.; Cai, C.; Lin, H., Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging. Angew Chem Int Ed Engl 2015, 54 (18), 5360-5363.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots with two-photon fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lu, S.; Sui, L.; Liu, J.; Zhu, S.; Chen, A.; Jin, M.; Yang, B., Near-Infrared Photoluminescent Polymer-Carbon Nanodots with Two-Photon Fluorescence. Advanced Materials 2017, 29 (15), 1603443.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared emissive carbon dots for two-photon fluorescence bioimaging
